blocks.text: JavaScript can do math just like a calculator. You already know +, -, *, and /. But there are a few extra tools that are extremely useful in games and real programs.

blocks.desc: The remainder operator — divides 10 by 3 and gives back what's left over (1). Useful for checking if something is even or odd.
A shortcut for score = score + 10. You can do this with any operator: +=, -=, *=, /=
An even shorter shortcut — adds exactly 1. score-- subtracts 1. You'll see these everywhere.

lines: score += 5;   // same as: score = score + 5   → score is 5
score *= 2;   // same as: score = score * 2   → score is 10
score++;      // same as: score = score + 1   → score is 11
score--;      // same as: score = score - 1   → score is 10
// Order of operations (same as regular math)

blocks.label: The % operator
blocks.text: The remainder (%) is one of the most useful tools in game programming. If score % 2 equals 0, score is even. If it equals 1, score is odd. You'll use this more than you expect.

exercises.title: Predict the math
tasks: Before running anything, calculate each result in your head or on paper:
Write your predictions down.
Then type each one in the editor with console.log to check.
exercises.hint: Remember: multiplication happens before addition unless you use parentheses.

exercises.title: Shortcut operators
tasks: Declare a variable called lives and set it to 3.
Use lives-- to lose a life. Log lives after.
Declare a variable called coins set to 0.
Use coins += 10 three times to collect 10 coins each time. Log after each.
Use coins *= 2 to double your coins. Log the final result.
exercises.hint: lives-- is the same as lives = lives - 1. coins += 10 is the same as coins = coins + 10.

checks.name: coins
checks.label: coins equals 60 after collecting 30 and doubling

exercises.title: Even or odd?
tasks: Declare a variable called num and set it to 7.
Log the result of num % 2.
Change num to 8 and log num % 2 again.
What does % 2 returning 0 mean? What does it returning 1 mean?
Try a few more numbers and confirm your rule.
exercises.hint: Any number % 2 gives you either 0 or 1. Think about what each one means.

checks.label: logged a result of 0 for an even number

exercises.title: Score calculator
tasks: Build a small score calculator from scratch.
Start with score = 0.
Add 50 points using +=.
Multiply by 3 using *=.
Subtract 25 using -=.
Add 1 point using ++.
Log 'Final score: ' + score at the end.
Expected final score: 126
exercises.hint: Do each operation one at a time and log after each step to track the value.

challenges.title: Why is the answer 23 instead of 32?
challenges.description: The code should calculate (2 + 3) * 4 and get 20, then add 3 to get 23. But the programmer expected 32. Find out why they're confused and explain it.
challenges.hint: Think about order of operations. What runs first?

checks.name: result
checks.label: result is 17 (the actual correct answer with order of operations)

challenges.explanation: 3 * 4 runs first (gives 12), then 2 + 12 + 3 = 17. Not 23, and not 32. To force a different order, use parentheses: (2 + 3) * 4 + 3 = 23.

challenges.title: The damage calculator is off
challenges.description: This game gives double damage when the hit number is even. But the damage is always being doubled. Fix it so it only doubles when hitNumber % 2 is 0.
challenges.hint: You haven't learned conditionals yet — but think: what should determine whether damage doubles? What's the value of hitNumber % 2 when hitNumber is 7?

checks.name: damage
checks.label: damage stays at 10 when hitNumber is odd

challenges.explanation: hitNumber is 7 — an odd number. 7 % 2 equals 1, not 0. So the double damage should NOT apply. The fix: remove the damage *= 2 line (for now). When you learn conditionals next, you'll be able to make it check automatically.
